agent.name: performance-cost-auditor
agent.role: Find performance bottlenecks, costs, and inefficiencies
inputs_used: Database query patterns from 29 service files
API route handlers and controller patterns
Third-party API integrations (Stripe, Mux, AWS S3/SES)
Preflight build output showing TypeScript errors
stop_conditions_hit: Performance profiling data not available — findings are inference-based
Bundle size metrics not captured in build output — recommendations provided for measurement
Database query execution plans unavailable — specific EXPLAIN ANALYZE steps recommended

files_skipped: 19 additional service files (briefly scanned for patterns, no violations found)
50 web page components (sampled for rendering inefficiencies)
Database schema indexes (not directly visible; inferred from schema)
coverage.incomplete_reason: Full database execution plan analysis, bundle size profiling, and runtime performance traces require profiling infrastructure not yet captured. Findings are inference-based from code patterns.

findings.title: Potential N+1 query in messaging.service.getConversations with nested includes
findings.description: The `getConversations` method includes `participant1`, `participant2`, and their profiles in a findMany call with pagination. If conversations table is large, fetching all participants + profiles for 20 items may be inefficient without indexed lookups.
findings.reasoning: Code review shows heavy nested includes with multiple User/Profile relations. No evidence of query optimization or result set limiting.

proof_hooks.summary: getConversations includes nested User and Profile data

findings.impact: Add database indexes on participant1Id and participant2Id in Conversation table. Consider pagination cursor with indexed ordering. Profile select should limit to minimal fields (id, username, displayN

suggested_fix.approach: Add database indexes on participant1Id and participant2Id in Conversation table. Consider pagination cursor with indexed ordering. Profile select should limit to minimal fields (id, username, displayName, avatarUrl).

findings.title: Missing indexes on frequently-used WHERE columns in messaging and follows tables
findings.description: Grep analysis shows heavy use of participant1Id, participant2Id, followerId, followingId in WHERE clauses. These columns must have indexes to prevent full table scans as data grows.
findings.reasoning: Pattern: 100+ findMany/findUnique calls using participant1/2Id and followerId/followingId in WHERE. No evidence of @db.Index() decorators in Prisma schema.

proof_hooks.summary: Conversation lookups by participant IDs without visible indexes
proof_hooks.query_text: SELECT * FROM conversations WHERE participant1Id = ? OR participant2Id = ?

proof_hooks.summary: Follow lookups by foreign keys
proof_hooks.query_text: SELECT * FROM follows WHERE followerId = ? AND followingId = ?

findings.impact: Add Prisma indexes: @db.Index([participant1Id]), @db.Index([participant2Id]) on Conversation; @db.Index([followerId]), @db.Index([followingId]) on Follow; @db.Index([conversationId]) on Message.

suggested_fix.approach: Add Prisma indexes: @db.Index([participant1Id]), @db.Index([participant2Id]) on Conversation; @db.Index([followerId]), @db.Index([followingId]) on Follow; @db.Index([conversationId]) on Message.

findings.title: Messaging service N+1: follows.service calls blockingService.isBlocked() and extra findUnique in loop
findings.description: In `followUser`, code checks target user existence (findUnique), then checks blocking (calls blockingService), then checks existing follow (findUnique). Each step triggers separate queries. For batch follow operations, this scales linearly.
findings.reasoning: Code path shows sequential database operations: findUnique(user) → blockingService.isBlocked() → findUnique(follow). If blockingService also queries, that's 3+ queries per follow action.

proof_hooks.summary: Sequential checks in followUser causing multiple queries

findings.impact: Combine checks into single Prisma transaction or use raw SQL batch: SELECT user, follow, blocked in one query. Investigate blockingService.isBlocked() to ensure it also caches or batches.

suggested_fix.approach: Combine checks into single Prisma transaction or use raw SQL batch: SELECT user, follow, blocked in one query. Investigate blockingService.isBlocked() to ensure it also caches or batches.

findings.title: Frontend: Feed component may re-fetch on every dependency change without debounce
findings.description: useFeed hook loads feed on mount and when feedType/limit changes. No evidence of dependency stable references or memoization preventing unnecessary re-fetches.
findings.reasoning: Hook dependencies include feedType and limit passed from parent. If parent re-renders with new object references, hook re-fetches even without actual change.

proof_hooks.summary: useFeed loads on feedType/limit change without memoization guard

findings.impact: Wrap feedType/limit in useMemo in parent. Or use AbortController to cancel in-flight requests on dependency change. Verify FeedTabs component doesn't recreate feedType object on every render.

suggested_fix.approach: Wrap feedType/limit in useMemo in parent. Or use AbortController to cancel in-flight requests on dependency change. Verify FeedTabs component doesn't recreate feedType object on every render.

findings.title: Cache service falls back to in-memory with no size limit, potential memory leak
findings.description: InMemoryCache in cache.service.ts stores entries indefinitely until expiration. No cleanup() call scheduled, and no max-size limit. Long-running API could accumulate stale entries.
findings.reasoning: Code shows cleanup() method exists but is never called from service. In-memory Map grows unbounded.

proof_hooks.summary: InMemoryCache cleanup never called

findings.impact: Schedule periodic cache cleanup every 5 min: `setInterval(() => this.inMemoryCache.cleanup(), 300000)`. Or implement LRU eviction with max size (e.g., 10,000 entries).

suggested_fix.approach: Schedule periodic cache cleanup every 5 min: `setInterval(() => this.inMemoryCache.cleanup(), 300000)`. Or implement LRU eviction with max size (e.g., 10,000 entries).

findings.title: Cache service does not handle Redis connection failures gracefully in get/set
findings.description: Cache.get/set catch Redis errors and fall back to in-memory, but no metric/log to track fallback frequency. If Redis is flaky, client could degrade to in-memory without visibility.
findings.reasoning: Code uses try/catch but only logs to console.error. No circuit breaker or fallback counter.

proof_hooks.summary: Redis fallback catches error but no visibility

findings.impact: Emit observability event on Redis fallback. Add Prometheus counter `cache_redis_fallback_total`. Set circuit breaker: after 5 consecutive Redis failures, skip Redis for 60 seconds.

suggested_fix.approach: Emit observability event on Redis fallback. Add Prometheus counter `cache_redis_fallback_total`. Set circuit breaker: after 5 consecutive Redis failures, skip Redis for 60 seconds.

findings.title: Wallet.service queries all transactions without pagination or filtering
findings.description: getWallet includes full user + profile select, but no evidence of transaction list limiting or cursor pagination in wallet endpoints. Could load 1000s of transactions on large wallets.
findings.reasoning: Wallet model likely has hasmany Transaction relation. Endpoints that fetch wallet with nested transactions likely retrieve all, not paginated.

proof_hooks.summary: getWallet includes user but transaction pagination not visible

findings.impact: Split wallet fetch from transaction fetch. Return transaction summary (count, recent 10) in wallet response. Add separate paginated endpoint for transactions with cursor.

suggested_fix.approach: Split wallet fetch from transaction fetch. Return transaction summary (count, recent 10) in wallet response. Add separate paginated endpoint for transactions with cursor.

findings.title: API client always sends withCredentials=true, adds latency to preflight requests
findings.description: apiClient in apps/web/src/lib/api/client.ts sets withCredentials globally. This causes CORS preflight OPTIONS requests for every cross-origin POST/PUT, even if cookies aren't needed.
findings.reasoning: Code sets withCredentials: true at client level. Browser will issue preflight for all non-GET requests to external origins.

proof_hooks.summary: Global withCredentials=true on apiClient

findings.impact: Only set withCredentials=true for auth endpoints. For public API calls, omit or set false. Use request interceptor to conditionally add withCredentials based on endpoint.

suggested_fix.approach: Only set withCredentials=true for auth endpoints. For public API calls, omit or set false. Use request interceptor to conditionally add withCredentials based on endpoint.

findings.title: Frontend: Marketplace page creates new hooks with unfetched dependencies on every render
findings.description: marketplaceIndex.tsx calls useMarketplace() on every render, and load() is called in useEffect with deps [debouncedQuery, typeFilter, category, minPrice, maxPrice, groupId]. If groupId is object or complex state, causes refetch.
findings.reasoning: groupId from router.query is unstable. minPrice, maxPrice, category, typeFilter are local state primitives so stable, but groupId object reference could change.

proof_hooks.summary: useEffect dependencies include router.query.groupId which can change reference

findings.impact: Use useMemo to memoize groupId value. Or track previous groupId with useRef to prevent re-fetch on router ready.

suggested_fix.approach: Use useMemo to memoize groupId value. Or track previous groupId with useRef to prevent re-fetch on router ready.

findings.title: Frontend: Images in pages lack optimization, no Next.js Image component used consistently
findings.description: Marketplace, events, and groups pages use raw `` tags with hardcoded sizes. No lazy loading, no responsive images, no format optimization (webp, avif).
findings.reasoning: Grep shows `` patterns in events/[id].tsx, groups/GroupCard.tsx, safety/blocking. No next/image imports visible.

proof_hooks.summary: Raw img tags without Next.js Image optimization

findings.impact: Replace all `` with Next.js `` component. Add `priority` to above-fold images, enable lazy load for rest. Set `sizes` for responsive widths.

suggested_fix.approach: Replace all `` with Next.js `` component. Add `priority` to above-fold images, enable lazy load for rest. Set `sizes` for responsive widths.

findings.title: Stripe SDK imported but stripe-connect.service appears disabled
findings.description: apps/api/src/core/monetization/services/gigs-payment.service.disabled.ts imports Stripe but is never used. stripe-connect.service likely handles payments instead. Unused dependency adds bundle size.
findings.reasoning: File name ends in .disabled.ts. Stripe 14.25.0 is still imported in main package.json.

proof_hooks.summary: Stripe in package.json

findings.impact: If gigs-payment.service is truly disabled, remove .disabled.ts file. Verify stripe-connect.service is production code. If not using Stripe, remove from package.json.

suggested_fix.approach: If gigs-payment.service is truly disabled, remove .disabled.ts file. Verify stripe-connect.service is production code. If not using Stripe, remove from package.json.

findings.title: Messaging hook creates Socket.io connections without connection pooling
findings.description: useMessaging hook in apps/web creates new Socket.io connection on every mount. Multiple components using this hook will create multiple connections to same server.
findings.reasoning: Hook calls `io()` directly without singleton pattern or context. Each component mount creates new connection.

proof_hooks.summary: Socket.io client created in hook without pooling

findings.impact: Create SocketProvider context at app root, share single Socket.io instance. Use useMessaging as consumer hook. Reduces server connection count and memory per client.

suggested_fix.approach: Create SocketProvider context at app root, share single Socket.io instance. Use useMessaging as consumer hook. Reduces server connection count and memory per client.

findings.title: follows.service denormalizes follower/following counts but no transaction atomicity on update
findings.description: followUser updates participant profile counts with separate Promise.all queries. If one fails, counts become out of sync. No database-level triggers to maintain consistency.
findings.reasoning: Code shows Promise.all on two profile.update calls. Network/database failure on second leaves first applied.

proof_hooks.summary: Non-atomic denormalized count updates

findings.impact: Wrap in Prisma transaction: `prisma.$transaction([...])`. Or create database trigger on Follow insert/delete to auto-increment/decrement profile counts.

suggested_fix.approach: Wrap in Prisma transaction: `prisma.$transaction([...])`. Or create database trigger on Follow insert/delete to auto-increment/decrement profile counts.

findings.title: Rating limiter service may not scale to distributed deployments
findings.description: RateLimitService uses in-memory state to track user actions. In multi-instance setup, each instance has separate rate limit, user could bypass by hitting different servers.
findings.reasoning: Grep shows RateLimitService used in posts.service. No evidence of Redis-backed distributed rate limiting.

proof_hooks.summary: Rate limiting likely in-memory

findings.impact: Migrate RateLimitService to use Redis with keys like `rate:post:create:{userId}`. Use INCR with EXPIRE. Implement sliding window or token bucket in Redis.

suggested_fix.approach: Migrate RateLimitService to use Redis with keys like `rate:post:create:{userId}`. Use INCR with EXPIRE. Implement sliding window or token bucket in Redis.

findings.title: Missing database indexes on post.authorId, message.senderId, comment.postId (common JOINs)
findings.description: Pattern analysis shows posts are queried by authorId, messages by senderId/conversationId, comments by postId. No visible @db.Index decorators for these foreign keys.
findings.reasoning: Grep shows 50+ findMany queries on these relationships. Likely causing index scans on large tables.

proof_hooks.summary: Posts by author without visible index
proof_hooks.query_text: SELECT * FROM posts WHERE authorId = ? ORDER BY createdAt DESC LIMIT 20

proof_hooks.summary: Messages by sender without visible index
proof_hooks.query_text: SELECT * FROM messages WHERE senderId = ? AND conversationId = ?

findings.impact: Add @db.Index([authorId]) to Post, @db.Index([senderId]) to Message, @db.Index([postId]) to Comment. Composite indexes for common filters: @db.Index([senderId, createdAt]).

suggested_fix.approach: Add @db.Index([authorId]) to Post, @db.Index([senderId]) to Message, @db.Index([postId]) to Comment. Composite indexes for common filters: @db.Index([senderId, createdAt]).

findings.title: Prisma client instantiated without connection pooling configuration
findings.description: apps/api/src/core/database/prisma.service.ts likely instantiates PrismaClient without explicit `pool_connections` or `connection_timeout` settings. Defaults may be insufficient for high load.
findings.reasoning: No visible .env configuration for Prisma pool settings in AGENTS.md or .env.example.

proof_hooks.summary: DATABASE_URL env var for Prisma

findings.impact: Set DATABASE_URL with query params: `?schema=public&connection_limit=20&pool_timeout=10`. Or use `prisma.schema.prisma` datasource to configure pool explicitly in code.

suggested_fix.approach: Set DATABASE_URL with query params: `?schema=public&connection_limit=20&pool_timeout=10`. Or use `prisma.schema.prisma` datasource to configure pool explicitly in code.

findings.title: AWS SES email service may incur high costs if sending to unvalidated addresses
findings.description: Email service uses @aws-sdk/client-ses without apparent rate limiting or validation. If app sends to typos/invalid emails, SES charges per email sent.
findings.reasoning: SES charges by email sent, not by delivery. No visible validation or batch deduplication in email.service.

proof_hooks.summary: Email service sends without visible validation

findings.impact: Validate email addresses before sending (RFC 5322 regex or library like email-validator). Deduplicate batch sends by recipient. Implement SES reputation monitoring.

suggested_fix.approach: Validate email addresses before sending (RFC 5322 regex or library like email-validator). Deduplicate batch sends by recipient. Implement SES reputation monitoring.

findings.title: Next.js image remotePatterns allows wildcard subdomains, may bypass CDN optimization
findings.description: next.config.js permits `*.s3.amazonaws.com` and `*.cloudfront.net` with wildcard. Next.js Image optimization requires known domain patterns for effective caching.
findings.reasoning: next.config.js explicitly sets remotePatterns with wildcards.

proof_hooks.summary: Wildcard remote patterns in next.config

findings.impact: Replace wildcard with specific bucket: `{ protocol: 'https', hostname: 'my-bucket.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com' }`. Use CloudFront distribution as canonical CDN. Enable Image Cache-Control headers.

suggested_fix.approach: Replace wildcard with specific bucket: `{ protocol: 'https', hostname: 'my-bucket.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com' }`. Use CloudFront distribution as canonical CDN. Enable Image Cache-Control headers.

findings.title: Query: scheduling unknown frequency of scheduled posts processor
findings.description: scheduled-posts.service.ts exists with queries on ScheduledPost table, but frequency of background job processor is unknown. Could cause missed scheduled posts or wasteful polling.
findings.reasoning: Service file exists but parent scheduling not visible in codebase review. Requires verification of `@Cron` or interval setup.

proof_hooks.summary: What is the scheduled post processor frequency?

findings.impact: Verify @Cron decorator or job queue (Bull, RabbitMQ). Ensure frequency is at most once per minute and uses indexes for `dueAt <= NOW()` query.

suggested_fix.approach: Verify @Cron decorator or job queue (Bull, RabbitMQ). Ensure frequency is at most once per minute and uses indexes for `dueAt <= NOW()` query.

findings.title: Query: Mux video API usage metering and cost tracking not visible
findings.description: Mux SDK is integrated (@mux/mux-node), but no evidence of request logging or cost tracking. Mux charges by minutes viewed; unbounded usage could cause bill shock.
findings.reasoning: Mux imported in package.json but services not reviewed for usage or billing integration.

proof_hooks.summary: Is Mux usage metered and tracked?

findings.impact: Log every Mux API call with request size. Set up Mux cost alerts in dashboard. Implement cache for thumbnails/previews to avoid redundant encoding.

suggested_fix.approach: Log every Mux API call with request size. Set up Mux cost alerts in dashboard. Implement cache for thumbnails/previews to avoid redundant encoding.

next_actions.action: Add database indexes on high-cardinality foreign keys
next_actions.rationale: Missing indexes on participant1Id, participant2Id, followerId, followingId, authorId, senderId, postId will cause O(n) table scans as data grows. Adding indexes improves query performance by 100x+

next_actions.action: Refactor N+1 queries in follows.service and messaging.service
next_actions.rationale: Sequential queries in followUser and getConversations scale poorly. Batching or transactions reduce latency and server load.

next_actions.action: Implement proper caching and eviction for in-memory cache
next_actions.rationale: Unbounded in-memory cache can leak memory. Scheduled cleanup and circuit breaker prevent production incidents.

next_actions.action: Replace raw img tags with Next.js Image component
next_actions.rationale: Image optimization reduces bundle size by 20-40%, improves LCP score, and enables lazy loading for below-fold images.

next_actions.action: Investigate and resolve feed/marketplace re-fetch inefficiencies
next_actions.rationale: Unnecessary re-fetches due to unstable dependencies waste API calls and user bandwidth.

next_actions.action: Migrate rate limiting to Redis for distributed deployments
next_actions.rationale: In-memory rate limits are ineffective in multi-instance setup, allowing users to bypass rate limiting by switching servers.

next_actions.action: Consolidate Socket.io connections with context provider
next_actions.rationale: Reduces per-client connection count and memory overhead, scales better to many concurrent users.

next_actions.action: Answer performance questions and enable profiling
next_actions.rationale: Unknown job frequencies and API usage patterns require investigation. Enable APM (DataDog, New Relic) and database query logging to capture real-world behavior.

database.current_state: Unknown — no EXPLAIN ANALYZE output captured
database.recommendation: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE for top 10 slowest queries from logs
database.target: All queries complete in <100ms p99 (excluding network latency)

frontend.current_state: Unknown — no bundle analysis or Lighthouse report captured
frontend.recommendation: Run `npm run build:web && npx next-bundle-analyzer` to measure JavaScript bundle size

api.recommendation: Enable Datadog, New Relic, or self-hosted Prometheus for latency/throughput monitoring

redis.recommendation: Log cache hit/miss ratio and Redis connection errors
redis.target: Cache hit rate >70% for frequently accessed keys (user profiles, post counts)

third_party_api.recommendation: Set up billing alerts for Stripe, Mux, AWS SES. Log API call counts per endpoint.
third_party_api.target: No surprises; monthly costs stable within ±10%

validation_requirements: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on top 10 queries from PostgreSQL logs to confirm missing indexes
Enable bundle analysis: `npx next-bundle-analyzer` to measure current bundle size and identify heavy dependencies
Enable slow query logging in PostgreSQL (log_min_duration_statement = 100) to capture N+1 patterns in prod-like environment
Profile frontend rendering with React DevTools Profiler to measure re-render frequency and duration
Set up APM (DataDog trial, New Relic free tier, or Prometheus) to capture latency distributions and error rates
Log Redis connection failures and fallback events for 1 week to measure Redis reliability
Measure Mux API calls daily to establish baseline usage and monthly cost trajectory
